statement: Iceland's previous EU accession negotiations were conducted from 2010 to 2013. Össur Skarphéðinsson (Samfylkingin) served as Foreign Minister from 2009 to 2013 and led the accession process. The application was submitted on 16 July 2009 under PM Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (Samfylkingin–VG coalition). Negotiations progressed through 11 of 33 chapters before the incoming Sjálfstæðisflokkur–Framsóknarflokkur government under PM Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson effectively froze the process in 2013. Iceland formally withdrew its application on 12 March 2015 under Foreign Minister Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson (Framsóknarflokkur).
source_name: Althingi — Historical records; European Commission accession records
caveats: The legal status of the 2015 withdrawal has been debated, as Althingi did not formally vote on withdrawing the application — the government acted unilaterally. Some argue the original application remains technically open. The current government's approach is to hold a new referendum rather than resume the old application.

related_entries.statement: The current Icelandic government is a coalition of Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), holding 32 of 63 Althingi seats. The Prime Minister is Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin). This is the first government coalition where both explicitly pro-EU parties (Samfylkingin and Viðreisn) serve together, which has been cited as a factor in advancing the EU referendum.

Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) holds 13 Althingi seats in session 157, tied as the largest party. Samfylkingin is pro-EU and leads the current government with Kristrún Frostadóttir as Prime Minister. The party submitted Iceland's EU membership application in 2009 under PM Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir. Current MPs: Kristrún Frostadóttir (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi norður), Alma D. Möller (Suðvesturkjördæmi), Dagbjört Hákonardóttir (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi norður), Logi Einarsson (Norðausturkjördæmi), Sigmundur Ernir Rúnarsson (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi norður), Jóhann Páll Jóhannsson (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi suður), Kristján Þórður Snæbjarnarson (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi suður), Guðmundur Ari Sigurjónsson (Suðvesturkjördæmi), Þórunn Sveinbjarnardóttir (Suðvesturkjördæmi), Arna Lára Jónsdóttir (Norðvesturkjördæmi), Víðir Reynisson (Suðurkjördæmi), Ása Berglind Hjálmarsdóttir (Suðurkjördæmi), Eydís Ásbjörnsdóttir (Norðausturkjördæmi).

Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) and Viðreisn (Reform Party) are the two Althingi parties that formally support EU membership. Samfylkingin has supported EU membership since 2007 and led the 2009 application process while in government. Viðreisn was founded in 2016 partly on a pro-EU platform and has consistently advocated for completing accession negotiations. Together they held approximately 17 of 63 Althingi seats after the 2024 elections.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Aðildarviðræður Jóhönnu Sigurðardóttur og Steingríms J. Sigfússonar steytti á skeri; þau gátu ekki klárað málið og gátu ekki farið með það í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu vegna þess að þau sáu að þau fengju ekki þær undanþágur sem ríkisstjórnarflokkarnir nú halda að þeir fái.

citations.report_title: Ekki víst hvernig Evrópusambandið bregst við

claims.claim_text: Ísland lagði inn aðildarumsókn til Evrópusambandsins árið 2010.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Erna Bjarnadóttir hagfræðingur átti sæti í þremur samningahópum stjórnvalda í aðildarviðræðunum við Evrópusambandið á árunum 2010 til 2013.
Alls var 11 samningaköflum lokað tímabundið í aðildarviðræðum Íslands við ESB.
